lesson.name: Rhetoric and First-Year Writing - Lesson 2: Writing Rhetorically
lesson.pdfName: Writing Rhetorically: Framing First Year Writing

sources.title: Writing Rhetorically: Framing First Year Writing

messages.content:
Based on the provided text, *Writing Rhetorically: Framing First-Year Writing* is a comprehensive resource designed to provide students with the foundational building blocks of academic composition. 

Here is a summary of the key points:

### **Purpose and Approach**
*   **Rhetorical Focus:** The course moves students beyond basic writing by teaching them to think rhetorically—viewing writing as a series of deliberate "standard moves" and intellectual tasks essential for academic inquiry (p. 11).
*   **Writing as a Process:** The text frames writing as a structured, chronological process (prewriting, drafting, revising, editing, and proofreading) rather than a task to be completed overnight (p. 11).
*   **Analytical Skill-Building:** Students learn to break complex ideas into smaller parts and evaluate relationships between them, a skill deemed necessary for college success and everyday life (p. 11).

### **Curriculum Structure**
*   **Rhetorical Modes:** The course is organized around eight specific rhetorical modes (e.g., Narrative, Description, Definition, Cause-Effect, Argument) paired across four major writing assignments (p. 11).
*   **Academic Scaffolding:** Each project builds on the last, starting with personal narrative and moving toward more complex, abstract analytical and persuasive tasks (p. 11).

### **Course Components**
*   **Integrated Support:** The book includes pedagogical tools such as assignment sheets, student worksheets, rubrics, and grammar/mechanics mini-lessons (pp. 5, 8, 28).
*   **Assessment:** Progress is measured through "low-stakes" formative assessments (discussion questions/exercises) and "high-stakes" summative assessments (four major essays) (p. 28).
*   **Accessibility:** Created for both traditional and dual-enrollment students, the resource emphasizes clear, logical organization and provides specific checklists and rubrics to ensure students understand how their work is evaluated (pp. 12, 13, 31).

Lesson 2: Writing Rhetorically - Presentation4 slides
Lesson 2: Writing Rhetorically
  • Master essential academic inquiry tools.
  • Learn to frame writing for clear, effective purpose.

The provided text introduces the textbook *Writing Rhetorically: Framing First Year Writing*, which is a collaborative project edited by Kirk Fontenot, Shelly Rodrigue, Wanda M. Waller, and Will Rogers [S.3]. 

Key points regarding the publication include:

*   Scope: The text is designed as a resource for first-year writing courses [S.3].
*   Origins and Adaptation: The textbook is an Open Educational Resource (OER) that was heavily adapted from *English Composition I: Rhetorical Methods-Based*, originally developed by Lumen Learning [S.4].
*   Licensing and Attribution: The content released by LOUIS: The Louisiana Library Network is governed by a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, with individual chapters noting specific sources or adapted content where applicable [S.4].

This textbook, titled *Writing Rhetorically: Framing First Year Writing*, serves as an open educational resource (OER) designed to teach essential academic writing skills through four major assignments. Each assignment integrates two complementary rhetorical modes to help students master the foundational tools required for college-level inquiry (p. 11).

Key aspects of the book include:

1. Writing as a Process: The text emphasizes that writing is a multi-stage activity rather than an overnight task. It provides specific chapters on invention, arrangement, drafting, revising, editing, and proofreading to help students plan effectively (p. 6, 11).

2. Structural Approach: The curriculum is organized around eight rhetorical modes, including narrative, description, definition, illustration, compare/contrast, evaluation, cause and effect, and argument (p. 7). Students advance from basic tasks like narrative and description to more complex analyses involving evaluation and argumentative cause-and-effect relationships (p. 11).

3. Learning Support: To assist students in mastering these concepts, the textbook provides comprehensive rubrics, student worksheets, and grammar mini-lessons (p. 5, 8). It encourages students to consult these rubrics frequently to understand how their work is evaluated and to maintain open communication with their professors (p. 12).

4. Project Background: Created through the Interactive OER for Dual Enrollment project, the book is designed to support both high school students and dual-enrollment learners. It has undergone rigorous review processes to ensure accessibility, appropriateness for the audience, and effective curriculum design (p. 13-14). 

5. Adaptability: The text is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License, allowing for free use and adaptation by other educators (p. 3, 15).

This document outlines the course syllabus and expectations for CENL 1013: English Composition I.

**Course Overview and Goals**
The course introduces students to the writing process, rhetorical strategies, and critical thinking [S.22]. There are six primary Student Learning Objectives (SLOs) focused on the multi-step writing process, audience awareness, essay organization, persuasive rhetoric, evidence integration, and grammatical conventions [S.23].

**Curriculum and Schedule**
The curriculum is divided into 15 modules covering various rhetorical methods, including:
* Writing foundations: Invention, arrangement, drafting, and proofreading [S.20].
* Composition styles: Narrative, description, definition, illustration, comparison/contrast, evaluation, cause/effect, and argument [S.20, S.24-26].
* Grammar: Mini-lessons on mechanics such as sentence types, comma usage, and parallelism [S.20, S.21].

**Assessment and Grading**
The course grade is determined by four major writing assignments (20% each), attendance (5%), and ongoing formative work, such as discussion questions and "Your Turn" exercises (15%) [S.28].
* Assignments: Students must submit a rough draft for every paper one week prior to the final deadline [S.28].
* Grading Criteria: Work is evaluated based on content, development, structure, formatting (MLA), and grammar [S.31, S.33-36].

**Policies and Requirements**
* Technology: Students must have consistent internet access, a word processor (MS Word .docx format), and familiarity with the school’s Learning Management System [S.27].
* Communication: Instructors aim to respond to student inquiries within 24 hours [S.23].
* Writing Standards: The first major assignment (Narrative and Description) requires a 2–4 page double-spaced essay in Times New Roman 12-point font, adhering to MLA formatting standards [S.32].

This document outlines the expectations, grading criteria, and writing strategies for three specific essay assignments: Definition/Exemplification, Compare and Evaluate, and Cause-Effect/Argumentation.

**General Guidelines for All Assignments**
Across all papers of this length (3-5 pages), students are expected to follow MLA formatting, utilize third-person perspective (explicitly required for the latter two assignments), and maintain logical organization. Strong essays should move beyond simple listing to provide nuanced analysis and clearly defined "so what" arguments (p. 49, 50, 55).

**Definition and Exemplification (p. 47)**
This assignment focuses on defining a term by emphasizing its meaning through specific examples. Researched topics require the use of library databases rather than the open web and mandate the use of in-text citations and a Works Cited page. Non-researched topics rely solely on personal experience.

**Compare and Evaluate (p. 48-52)**
The goal is to analyze two related subjects by examining their similarities and differences to gain a more profound understanding of why they are grouped together.
1. Success depends on selecting subjects that are similar enough to be comparable but different enough to sustain an argument (p. 50).
2. The essay should avoid list-like structures in favor of a cohesive narrative that highlights the significance (the "so what") of the comparison (p. 50, 52).
3. Grading is heavily weighted toward organization (30%) and the clarity of the thesis (20%) (p. 49).

**Cause-Effect and Argument (p. 53-56)**
This assignment requires students to support a debatable claim by identifying relevant causes and effects.
1. The thesis must be a provable, debatable assertion rather than a factual statement or a simple announcement (p. 53, 55, 56).
2. Credibility is established by avoiding fallacious reasoning, acknowledging opposing viewpoints, and providing honest evidence (p. 55).
3. Secondary sources are optional; however, if used, they must be documented in MLA format (p. 53, 56).
4. Evaluations prioritize the strength of the thesis, the quality of supporting evidence, and logical progression (p. 54).

This document outlines the essential structural and stylistic requirements for an argumentative paper, focusing on the rhetorical elements necessary to build a persuasive case.

Formatting and Organization
Papers must follow MLA formatting, including double-spacing and proper documentation (p. 57). The paper must be organized into distinct sections:

1. Introduction: Must define the topic, explain the controversy, and clearly state the author's assertion (p. 57).
2. Background Information: Provides necessary context, explains key terms, and reaffirms the author's claim (p. 57).
3. Supporting Evidence: This section must be divided into three specific rhetorical appeals (p. 57):
Ethos: Establishing the author’s credibility and personal knowledge.
Logos: Presenting logical evidence, such as laws, constitutional rights, or financial facts.
Pathos: Appealing to the audience's emotions, values, or moral beliefs.
4. Counterargument and Refutation: The author must fairly summarize opposing views and then refute them to strengthen their own position (p. 57).
5. Conclusion: The final section should restate the assertion, summarize key points, and offer a specific recommendation or course of action (p. 58).

Evaluation
The text provides a checklist for students to ensure their work meets these academic standards. Key questions include verifying if the thesis is debatable, confirming that all three rhetorical appeals are included in separate paragraphs, and ensuring that all secondary sources are cited using MLA protocols (p. 58). Finally, students are encouraged to proofread for mechanical and grammatical errors before submission (p. 58).

The section covering Chapter 3, titled "Drafting and Revising" (p. 65-74), outlines the essential stages of the writing process following initial invention and arrangement. The key points are summarized below:

The writing process is framed as a structured, chronological sequence rather than a chaotic, spontaneous event. By viewing writing as a series of defined stages, students can plan their work more efficiently and reduce the stress associated with drafting.

Drafting is presented as the opportunity to translate ideas and organizational plans into a coherent text. This stage focuses on getting thoughts onto paper, allowing the writer to see how arguments and narratives take shape through prose.

Revision is identified as a critical intellectual task that involves moving beyond surface-level changes. It requires the writer to analyze their work, examine the relationship between parts, and refine their ideas to better meet the requirements of the assignment. This process is iterative, meaning writers should expect to revisit and transform their drafts multiple times to achieve clarity and depth.

The chapter emphasizes that utilizing these stages—moving from invention to arrangement, and finally to drafting and revising—provides a reliable framework for academic success, helping students build the analytical skills necessary for complex, college-level inquiry.

### Summary of Chapters: Editing, Proofreading, and Rhetorical Modes (pp. 75-190)

The text outlines technical strategies for refining written work and provides detailed instruction on specific rhetorical modes essential for academic composition.

**Refining the Writing Process**
*   **Editing and Proofreading (p. 75):** This stage shifts the focus from structural content to the mechanics of writing. It emphasizes correcting errors in grammar, punctuation, and syntax to ensure professional clarity and readability.

**Core Rhetorical Modes**
*   **Narrative and Description (pp. 83, 95):** These chapters explore the use of descriptive language to convey sequence and imagery. Narrative centers on storytelling, while description utilizes sensory details to create vivid depictions.
*   **Definition and Illustration (pp. 105, 127):** These sections instruct students on how to unpack complex concepts. Definition clarifies terminology, while illustration/example provides the necessary evidence to ground abstract ideas in concrete reality.
*   **Compare/Contrast and Evaluation (pp. 145, 161):** These chapters focus on analytical skills. The compare/contrast mode identifies relationships between subjects, and evaluation requires students to establish specific criteria to weigh the merits and weaknesses of two entities.

**Strategic Frameworks**
The pedagogical approach assumes that mastery of these modes requires both abstract thinking and the application of standardized academic moves. By utilizing the writing process as a series of chronological stages rather than a singular event, students can move from foundational assignments to more complex, multi-layered analytical tasks.

The section covering Chapter 12: Argument (pp. 191-204) focuses on developing the skills necessary to construct persuasive academic arguments. The key points are structured below:

Definition and Purpose
Argumentation in an academic context is defined as a process of critical inquiry rather than a simple disagreement or confrontation. Its primary purpose is to persuade an audience by presenting a logical case supported by evidence, rather than relying solely on emotion or personal opinion. The text emphasizes that argumentation is essential for sophisticated intellectual engagement with complex topics.

Core Components of an Argument
A successful argument requires several foundational elements:
1. A clear, debatable thesis statement that defines the writer's position.
2. Supporting evidence, which may include data, expert testimony, or specific examples, to validate the claims made.
3. Analysis, which connects the evidence to the thesis, ensuring the reader understands how the proof supports the argument.
4. Consideration of counterarguments, which involves acknowledging opposing viewpoints and providing a rebuttal to strengthen the writer's original position.

The Rhetorical Approach
The text encourages students to view the writing of an argument as a rhetorical task. Students are prompted to evaluate their audience, recognize their own perspective, and structure their reasoning so that it is accessible and logical. This aligns with the broader goals of the textbook, which seeks to teach students how to break down complex issues into manageable, logical parts.

Integration of Skills
This chapter acts as a culmination of the skills learned in previous chapters. By this stage, students are expected to utilize previously studied rhetorical modes—such as cause and effect, definition, and evaluation—to bolster their arguments. The chapter guides students through the transition from exploring a topic to asserting a formal stance, encouraging a methodical process of drafting and refining their claims.

The provided text (pages 205–232) covers essential technical skills for academic writing, focusing on research documentation, grammar, sentence structure, and style. The key points are organized as follows:

MLA Research and Citation (p. 205): This chapter provides guidance on using the Modern Language Association (MLA) format, which is standard for research papers. It covers the mechanics of properly citing sources to maintain academic integrity and support arguments with credible evidence.

Grammar and Mechanics Mini-Lessons (p. 219): This section offers targeted instruction on fundamental writing errors and rules. Topics included are:

- Sentence Structure: Managing subjects, verbs, irregular verbs, and subject-verb agreement (p. 220).
- Sentence Types: Differentiating between various sentence structures (p. 221).
- Error Correction: Addressing common mistakes such as fragments (p. 222), run-on sentences, and comma splices (p. 223).
- Punctuation and Formatting: Guidelines for comma usage (p. 225), the placement of apostrophes (p. 227), and proper capitalization (p. 228).
- Clarity: Strategies for maintaining parallel structure in writing (p. 226).

Language Variety and Style (p. 230): The final section covers the concept of "De Copia" (demonstrating the variety of language) and provides specific exercises to help students refine their writing voice. This emphasizes that effective writing involves not just grammatical accuracy, but also a deliberate command of language and tone.

These chapters serve as a practical toolkit for students to refine their drafts, ensure their technical writing skills meet institutional standards, and develop a more sophisticated and varied writing style.

The requested section corresponds to the end of the textbook, focusing on accessibility, terminology, and supplementary resources.

Key points regarding the content on pages 233–256:

Appendix A: Checklist for Accessibility (p. 233)
This section provides essential criteria for ensuring that course materials are usable by all students. It emphasizes removing barriers to learning, which is a requirement for the open educational resources (OER) developed for this project.

Glossary (p. 236)
A reference guide is provided to define key rhetorical and academic terms utilized throughout the text. This serves as a quick-reference tool for students to look up specific vocabulary related to writing processes and rhetorical modes.

Supplementary Content
Building on the earlier chapters regarding grammar and mechanics (starting on p. 219), these final pages serve to reinforce academic standards. The text encourages students to view writing as a structured, chronological process rather than an unorganized task. By utilizing these checklists and definitions, students are expected to move toward greater proficiency in analytical writing and critical inquiry.

Overall Purpose
The concluding pages align with the book’s mission to support dual-enrollment students by providing scaffolding and clear standards for quality curriculum design. These resources ensure that all students—regardless of their prior experience—have access to the tools necessary for succeeding in college-level composition.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the 'About This Book' section, why is it advantageous for students to follow a structured, multi-stage writing process rather than just starting to write?
options: It eliminates the need for students to conduct any research.
It ensures the paper will reach a minimum required word count.
It allows the instructor to grade the paper at each individual stage.
It provides a clear roadmap that is more efficient than chaotic, unorganized drafting.
questions.explanation: The text explains that viewing writing as a process with defined stages and goals is faster and more efficient than muddling through a chaotic process.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose behind requiring students to use specific 'rhetorical modes' in their assignments throughout the semester?
options: To force students to write about specific topics assigned by the department.
To test whether students can identify which rhetorical mode an author is using in professional readings.
To help students master standard intellectual 'moves' essential for complex inquiry and college success.
To ensure students can memorize the rigid definitions of different essay types.
questions.explanation: The text states that these modes represent 'a set of standard moves that are vital to critical inquiry' and help students develop essential intellectual tasks.

questions.question: An assignment asks students to identify specific criteria to assess and compare two different items. Which cognitive skill does the text suggest this activity develops?
options: The ability to break complex subjects into parts and understand the relationships between them (analysis).
The ability to identify grammatical errors in peer writing.
The ability to prioritize personal opinion over objective evidence.
The ability to narrate personal experiences with descriptive language.
questions.explanation: The text connects evaluation and comparison to the challenging practice of analysis, defined here as breaking things into parts and finding relationships between them.

questions.question: Why does the course include both a narrative paper and a cause-and-effect argument paper at different points in the semester?
options: Because narrative writing is easier to grade than argumentative writing.
To scaffold learning, moving from basic structures of thought to more complex, multi-layered intellectual tasks.
To ensure all students follow the same writing speed regardless of their prior experience.
To ensure students spend an equal amount of time on creative writing and technical writing.
questions.explanation: The text notes that early assignments prepare students for later, more complex tasks, indicating that the curriculum is scaffolded to build upon previous skills.

questions.question: Which of these best reflects the textbook's approach to the role of the professor?
options: The professor is responsible for providing all answers, as students are not expected to think abstractly.
The professor is a secondary contact, as the textbook is designed to be fully self-taught.
The professor is an objective evaluator who should not be contacted by students once grading begins.
The professor is a resource for help who depends on students to proactively communicate if they are struggling.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states: 'don’t hesitate to contact your professor... They’re not psychic, and if you’re struggling, it never hurts to let them know what’s going on.'

cards.front: What is the primary pedagogical goal of 'Writing Rhetorically'?
cards.back: To teach students the standard rhetorical 'moves' and building blocks of academic writing through major assignments that combine complementary rhetorical modes.

cards.front: List the four major writing assignments in the course.
cards.back: 1. Narrative and Description, 2. Definition and Exemplification, 3. Compare and Evaluate, 4. Cause-Effect and Argument.

cards.front: Why does the textbook emphasize writing as a process rather than a one-time event?
cards.back: It provides a chronological, efficient structure (prewriting, drafting, revising, editing) that helps students move from abstract thought to a final draft without feeling overwhelmed.

cards.front: Define the rhetorical mode of 'analysis' as used in this course.
cards.back: The challenging practice of breaking a complex subject into parts and identifying the relationships between those components.

cards.front: What does SLO 1 (Student Learning Objective 1) regarding the writing process entail?
cards.back: Adopting a multi-step, recursive process that includes prewriting, drafting, revising, editing, and proofreading.

cards.front: What is the purpose of the 'rubric' provided with each major assignment?
cards.back: To clarify how an assignment will be scored or evaluated, serving as a key guide for student success.
The opportunity for a student to be enrolled in high school and college simultaneously.

cards.front: What is the primary goal of the 'Compare and Evaluate' paper?
cards.back: To identify specific criteria (individual traits) to assess two things and determine their relative merits and weaknesses.

cards.front: What is the focus of the final paper (Assignment 4)?
cards.back: To describe and make an argument about a cause-and-effect relationship, incorporating analytical skills developed throughout the semester.

cards.front: According to the course policies, what should you do if you are struggling with an assignment?
cards.back: Contact your professor immediately, as they are not psychic and need to be informed of your progress.

slides.title: Lesson 2: Writing Rhetorically
bullets: Learn to frame writing for clear, effective purpose.
slides.notes: In this lesson, we explore how to approach writing not just as an assignment, but as a framework for critical inquiry. By mastering rhetorical methods, you create a solid foundation for all your college-level academic work.

slides.title: Writing as a Process
bullets: Use defined stages: prewriting, drafting, revising, editing (p. 23).
slides.notes: Writing is a systematic process, not an overnight task. By following structured steps, you spend less time 'muddling through' and more time effectively planning and executing your ideas.

slides.title: Developing Rhetorical Modes
bullets: Master standard moves for college inquiry.
Practice narrative, definition, and evaluation methods (p. 11).
slides.notes: Your coursework introduces key rhetorical modes—like narrative and comparison. These assignments help you build the intellectual 'building blocks' necessary for sophisticated analysis and communication across any major.

slides.title: Structuring Your Argument
bullets: Establish credibility, logic, and emotional connection (p. 45).
Acknowledge counterarguments to strengthen your position (p. 45).
Conclude by restating your main claim.
slides.notes: Effective writing requires a balance of ethos, logos, and pathos. A well-structured essay doesn't just present a point; it anticipates counterarguments, showing fairness while reinforcing your primary assertion.
